1.lecture:
§1 · Numbers that carry units

Engineering mathematics begins one step before calculus: every quantity you will ever differentiate or integrate carries a unit, and the units obey algebra just as the numbers do. When a pump curve says head is 42 m, that number is meaningless until you know it multiplies through \( P = \rho g Q H \) to give watts:

\[ [P] = \frac{\mathrm{kg}}{\mathrm{m}^3}\cdot\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}^2}\cdot\frac{\mathrm{m}^3}{\mathrm{s}}\cdot \mathrm{m} = \frac{\mathrm{kg\,m^2}}{\mathrm{s}^3} = \mathrm{W} \tag{1} \]

Check the units first and half of all calculation errors die before they are born. This is the dimensional analysis discipline: both sides of any physical equation must carry identical dimensions, and arguments of \( \sin \), \( \ln \), \( e^x \) must be dimensionless.

§2 · Functions as machine behavior

A function \( f(x) \) is a machine's behavior written down: give it a flow, it returns a pressure drop; give it a time, it returns a temperature. The pump curve \( H(Q) \), the motor torque curve \( T(\omega) \), the cooling curve \( T(t) \) — the whole plant is a library of functions. Domain matters physically: \( H(Q) \) exists only for \( Q \ge 0 \), and beyond runout flow the curve is not merely undefined but forbidden.

§3 · Magnitudes and scales

An engineer estimates before calculating. Powers of ten are the estimating language: a 100 m³/h pump moves \(\sim\)28 L/s; a kilowatt is ten 100 W lamps; a bar is \(\sim\)10 m of water column. On the plant floor this means you can smell a wrong answer: if a small transfer pump computes to 300 kW, the arithmetic is wrong before you re-check it — the machine on the skid is the size of a suitcase, and suitcases don't dissipate 300 kW.

§4 · The unit-check working method

Knowing that units must balance is not the same as having a method. Use this three-step protocol on every equation you meet, in this course and every one after it. Step one: write the equation symbolically and substitute units, not numbers — run the algebra on the units alone and confirm both sides match, exactly as equation (1) did for pump power. Step two: convert every input to base SI before any arithmetic: flows to m³/s, pressures to Pa, lengths to m. Most real-world calculation errors are unit errors, and most unit errors happen when kPa meets Pa or m³/h meets m³/s in the same line. Step three: after computing, translate the answer back into the working units of the trade — kW, bar, m³/h — and test it against an order-of-magnitude estimate before you write it down.

Two traps deserve names. The gauge trap: gauge and absolute pressure differ by one atmosphere, and equations built on absolute pressure quietly fail when fed gauge readings. The prefix trap: a dropped factor of 1000 from k, M, or m survives every later step and grows more embarrassing the longer it lives. Scientific notation is the antidote — write \( 2.5\times10^5 \) Pa and exponents stay visible and checkable.

Closing. Units are algebra, functions are machine behavior, and order-of-magnitude sense is the cheapest error detector you will ever own. Every lesson that follows leans on all three.
1.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Algebraic manipulation — solving \( P=\rho g Q H \) for any one symbol.
  • SI prefixes — k, M, m, µ span nine orders of magnitude; conversion is exponent bookkeeping.
  • Scientific notation — \( 3.2\times10^4 \) beats 32000 for error-catching.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Dimensional homogeneity\( [\mathrm{LHS}] = [\mathrm{RHS}] \)Run on every equation before numbers go in; both sides must carry identical dimensions.
Hydraulic power\( P = \rho g Q H \)Turning a pump duty (flow, head) into watts — with \( Q \) in m³/s, never m³/h.
Pressure head\( h = P/(\rho g) \)Translating a pressure into a liquid column and back; 1 bar ≈ 10.2 m of water.
Unit conversion\( 1\ \mathrm{bar} = 100\ \mathrm{kPa} = 10^5\ \mathrm{Pa} \)Moving between gauge readings, datasheets, and SI calculations without a factor-of-1000 slip.
Function and domain\( H = H(Q),\ Q \ge 0 \)Reading curves as machine behavior; respect where the curve is physically forbidden.
Order of magnitude\( \sim 10^n \)The first sanity screen of any computed answer — suitcase-sized machines don't dissipate megawatts.
Dimensionless argument\( \sin(\cdot),\ \ln(\cdot),\ e^{(\cdot)} \)Whatever sits inside these functions must be a pure number.
1.kuwait:
Vignette 1 — the suspicious kilowatt. You arrive at a pump house during an energy audit and the datasheet claims a 15 kW motor delivers 260 m³/h at 35 m. Equation (1) says the water alone needs \( 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0722 \times 35 \approx 24.8\ \mathrm{kW} \) — more than the motor can give before efficiency. The datasheet is internally impossible; either the duty or the nameplate was transcribed wrong. Ten seconds of unit algebra just saved a warranty dispute.

Vignette 2 — reading a foreign drawing. A packaged unit arrives documented in psi and US gpm. Before the coordination meeting you convert the relief setting — 150 psi → 10.3 bar — and flag that the plant's 10 bar-rated header cannot legally receive it (ASME B31.3 pressure design basis). The catch costs one email now instead of a rework order later.

quiz.q:
Hydraulic and shaft power

Problem. A cooling-water pump at a Gulf petrochemical utility area delivers \( Q = 120\ \mathrm{m^3/h} \) of water against a total head \( H = 42\ \mathrm{m} \). Pump efficiency is 65%. Given: \( \rho = 1000\ \mathrm{kg/m^3},\ g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2} \). Find: hydraulic power and shaft power.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Convert flow: \( Q = 120/3600 = 0.0333\ \mathrm{m^3/s} \). (2) Hydraulic power: \( P_h = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0333 \times 42 = 13.7\ \mathrm{kW} \). (3) Shaft power: \( P_s = P_h/\eta = 13.7/0.65 = 21.1\ \mathrm{kW} \).

Answer: \( P_h \approx 13.7\ \mathrm{kW},\ P_s \approx 21.1\ \mathrm{kW} \). Sanity check: a 22 kW motor frame on a 120 m³/h duty is entirely ordinary — had we forgotten the 3600, we'd have gotten 49 MW, a power-station number.

quiz.q:
Unit conversion under pressure

Problem. A compressed-air header reads 6.0 bar(g). Find: the gauge pressure in kPa and psi, and the approximate equivalent water column.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( 6.0\ \mathrm{bar} = 600\ \mathrm{kPa} \). (2) \( 600 \times 0.145 = 87\ \mathrm{psi} \). (3) Water column: \( h = P/\rho g = 600{,}000 / (1000 \times 9.81) = 61.2\ \mathrm{m} \).

Answer: 600 kPa ≈ 87 psi ≈ 61 m H₂O. Sanity check: the 10 m-per-bar rule gives 60 m — our exact figure lands within 2%.

quiz.q:
Scaling a transmitter signal

Problem. A level transmitter on a 2.5 m measuring range outputs a linear 4–20 mA signal: 0 m maps to 4 mA, 2.5 m maps to 20 mA. Find: the function \( L(I) \) converting current to level, the level at a reading of 11.2 mA, and the function's physical domain.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) The span is 16 mA for 2.5 m, so \( L(I) = 2.5\,\dfrac{I - 4}{16} \) metres, with \( I \) in mA. (2) At \( I = 11.2 \): \( L = 2.5 \times 7.2/16 = 1.125\ \mathrm{m} \). (3) Domain: \( 4 \le I \le 20 \) mA — readings below 4 mA are not levels but fault signals, which is precisely why the live-zero convention exists.

Answer: \( L(I) = 2.5(I-4)/16 \); 1.125 m at 11.2 mA; domain 4–20 mA. Sanity check: 11.2 mA sits at \( 7.2/16 = 45\% \) of the current span, and 45% of 2.5 m is 1.125 m — the function agrees with the proportion.

quiz.q: A transfer pump delivers \( Q = 90\ \mathrm{m^3/h} \) of water (\( \rho = 1000\ \mathrm{kg/m^3} \)) against a total head \( H = 25\ \mathrm{m} \), with pump efficiency \( \eta = 75\% \). Taking \( g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2} \), what shaft power must the motor supply?
quiz.solution: Convert the flow first: \( Q = 90/3600 = 0.025\ \mathrm{m^3/s} \), so the hydraulic power is \( P_h = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 25 = 6.13\ \mathrm{kW} \), and the shaft power is \( P_s = P_h/\eta = 6.13/0.75 = 8.2\ \mathrm{kW} \). Efficiency divides rather than multiplies: the motor must supply more power than the water receives, which eliminates 4.6 kW (the classic multiply-by-\( \eta \) error) and 6.1 kW (efficiency ignored). The 29.4 MW figure comes from leaving \( Q \) in \( \mathrm{m^3/h} \) — an order-of-magnitude check rejects it at once, since a small transfer pump is not a power station.

quiz.q: A tank cooling curve is modelled as \( T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)\,e^{-t/\tau} \), where \( t \) is time in seconds. What does dimensional homogeneity require of the constant \( \tau \)?
choices: \( \tau \) must carry units of seconds, so that the exponent \( t/\tau \) is dimensionless
\( \tau \) must carry units of kelvin, to match the temperatures in the equation
\( \tau \) must be a pure number, since constants in physical equations are dimensionless
\( \tau \) may carry any units, because the exponential function converts them automatically
quiz.solution: The argument of \( e^x \) — like those of \( \sin \) and \( \ln \) — must be dimensionless, so \( \tau \) must share the dimension of \( t \): time. The kelvin answer commits the classic error of matching the constant to the equation's output units instead of to the argument it sits inside; the temperature dimensions are already balanced by the prefactor \( (T_0 - T_\infty) \). No function absorbs units, and constants in physical equations routinely carry dimensions — homogeneity is checked term by term and argument by argument.

quiz.q: A water pump moves \( 100\ \mathrm{m^3/h} \) against 30 m of head, with pump efficiency around 70%. Which shaft-power magnitude is plausible?
quiz.solution: Hydraulic power is \( \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (100/3600) \times 30 \approx 8.2\ \mathrm{kW} \); dividing by the 0.70 efficiency gives ≈12 kW of shaft power. The 120 W and 1.2 MW answers are the classic unit slips — forgetting the 3600 in the flow conversion moves the result by exactly these factors. Order-of-magnitude sense is the fastest error detector: a pump this size is a two-person lift, not a power-station item.

quiz.q: A chilled-water line gauge reads \( 250\ \mathrm{kPa} \). Using \( \rho = 1000\ \mathrm{kg/m^3} \) and \( g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2} \), what height of water column does this pressure correspond to?
quiz.solution: Convert to base units before substituting: \( h = P/(\rho g) = 250000/(1000 \times 9.81) = 25.5\ \mathrm{m} \). The rule of thumb that 1 bar is roughly 10 m of water confirms it: 250 kPa is 2.5 bar, hence about 25 m. The 0.025 m answer is the classic slip of feeding kPa into an SI formula without converting to Pa, and 250 m follows from misconverting 250 kPa into 25 bar — both fail the order-of-magnitude sanity check for a building chilled-water loop.

quiz.q: An energy report lists a consumption of 3.6 MJ. What is that in kilowatt-hours?
quiz.solution: One kilowatt-hour is 1000 W sustained for 3600 s: \( 1000 \times 3600 = 3.6\times10^6\ \mathrm{J} = 3.6\ \mathrm{MJ} \) — so 3.6 MJ is exactly 1.0 kWh. Choosing 3.6 kWh treats MJ and kWh as the same unit with different labels, the classic energy-billing slip; the two differ by precisely the 3600 seconds in an hour.

2.lecture:
§1 · Why limits exist in a plant

A limit answers one question: what value does a function approach as its input approaches a point? Formally, \( \lim_{x\to a} f(x) = L \) means we can force \( f(x) \) arbitrarily close to \( L \) by taking \( x \) close enough to \( a \). The definition sounds abstract; the plant meaning is concrete: what does the sensor settle toward, what does the ratio become, what happens at the operating edge?

§2 · The ratio that defines everything

The most important limit in this course is a ratio of vanishing quantities,

\[ \lim_{\theta\to 0}\frac{\sin\theta}{\theta} = 1 \tag{1} \]

Try it numerically: at \( \theta = 0.1 \) rad the ratio is 0.9983; at 0.01, 0.99998. The two quantities shrink together but their ratio converges — that is the whole mental model for the derivative in the next lesson, where a vanishing rise over a vanishing run converges to a slope.

§3 · Continuity — and what it means for signals

\( f \) is continuous at \( a \) when \( \lim_{x\to a} f(x) = f(a) \): no jump, no hole, no blow-up. Physical processes — temperature, level, pressure — are continuous; their measurements need not be. A thermocouple that jumps 40 °C between scans hasn't witnessed magic; it has witnessed electrical noise, a loose connection, or a step change in what is being touched. Knowing which physical variables can and cannot jump is a diagnostic tool: pressure can step (valve slam), metal temperature cannot.

One-sided limits close the lesson: a relay approaching its trip point from below and from above sees two different worlds, \( \lim_{x\to a^-} \) and \( \lim_{x\to a^+} \). Hysteresis is one-sided limits wearing work clothes.

§4 · How to evaluate a limit — the decision tree

Faced with a limit, work a fixed sequence. First, substitute. If the function is continuous there, the limit is the value and you are done — most limits in practice end here. Second, if substitution returns 0/0, the form is telling you a common factor is hiding: factor polynomials, or multiply by the conjugate when square roots appear, cancel, and substitute again. Third, check the sides. When the expression involves absolute values, piecewise definitions, or a physical threshold, compute the left and right limits separately; the two-sided limit exists only when they agree. Fourth, verify numerically. Evaluate the expression at inputs marching toward the point — 0.1, 0.01, 0.001 away — and watch the trend. This is not cheating; it is how an engineer confirms an algebraic result the same way a technician confirms a calibration: with converging evidence.

Use continuity as a diagnostic tool the same disciplined way. Before blaming a sensor or believing a spike, ask which physical variables can genuinely jump (pressure, force, current) and which are integrals of finite flows and therefore cannot (metal temperature, tank level, accumulated volume). The mathematics sorts plant events from instrument events before the first work order is written.

Closing. Limits formalize approach; continuity formalizes no surprises. Tomorrow they combine into the derivative.
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Function evaluation and graphs (Lesson 1) — reading \( f \) near a point, not just at it.
  • Radian measure — the sine limit is false in degrees; radians make arc = radius × angle.
  • Factoring — resolving \( \tfrac{x^2-9}{x-3} \) requires \( (x-3)(x+3) \).

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Limit\( \lim_{x\to a} f(x) = L \)The settling value; approach, not arrival — may exist where the function value doesn't.
The sine limit\( \lim_{\theta\to 0}\tfrac{\sin\theta}{\theta} = 1 \)Small-angle work and the derivative of sine; radians only.
The 0/0 formfactor, or multiply by the conjugateWhen substitution returns 0/0, a hidden common factor is waiting to cancel.
Continuity\( \lim_{x\to a} f(x) = f(a) \)Deciding whether a logged jump can be physical or must be instrumental.
One-sided limits\( x\to a^-,\ x\to a^+ \)Trip points, hysteresis, absolute values, piecewise behavior.
Removable discontinuityhole at \( x=a \)The limit exists; the definition merely misses the point.
2.kuwait: Vignette — the trip that "came from nowhere." You arrive at a compressor package that tripped on high discharge temperature. The trend shows a smooth 8 °C/min climb for six minutes, then trip — continuous, physical, real: a cooler problem, not a sensor problem. The operator insists the temperature "jumped." The one-second historian data says otherwise: nothing jumped; it approached. You write the finding around the trend's continuity and the fouled cooler is confirmed at inspection. Limits are how you argue with certainty about what a signal was doing just before the event.

quiz.q:
A hole, not a wall

Problem. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}\frac{x^2-9}{x-3} \), which appears as a 0/0 form. Find: the limit.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Factor: \( \frac{(x-3)(x+3)}{x-3} = x+3 \) for \( x \ne 3 \). (2) The simplified function is continuous, so the limit is \( 3+3 = 6 \).

Answer: 6. Sanity check: at \( x=3.001 \), the original ratio gives \( (9.006-9)/0.001 = 6.001 \) — converging on 6 as promised.

quiz.q:
Sensor step or process step?

Problem. During pasteurizer start-up at a dairy filling plant, the product-outlet RTD logs 71.8, 71.9, 71.9, 112.4, 72.0 °C at 1 s scan. Find: is the 112.4 reading a process event or an instrument event, and which continuity argument decides it?
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Product temperature obeys a thermal time constant of tens of seconds: its true trajectory is continuous and cannot traverse +40 °C and −40 °C within 2 s. (2) An instrument channel — connector, noise burst, scan glitch — can jump discontinuously. (3) Therefore the spike violates physical continuity but not signal behavior: instrument event.

Answer: single-scan instrument artifact; flag the channel, don't chase a process excursion. Sanity check: energy to heat the product stream 40 °C in 1 s would exceed the pasteurizer's entire installed heating duty by an order of magnitude.

quiz.q:
A limit that will become a slope

Problem. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{\sqrt{4+h}-2}{h} \), which substitution renders as 0/0. (Next lesson this exact limit will be recognized as the derivative of \( \sqrt{x} \) at \( x=4 \).)
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Multiply by the conjugate: \( \dfrac{\sqrt{4+h}-2}{h}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{4+h}+2}{\sqrt{4+h}+2} = \dfrac{(4+h)-4}{h(\sqrt{4+h}+2)} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{4+h}+2} \). (2) Substitute \( h = 0 \): \( 1/(2+2) = 1/4 \).

Answer: 1/4. Sanity check: at \( h = 0.01 \), \( (\sqrt{4.01}-2)/0.01 = 0.2498 \) — marching on 0.25 exactly as the algebra promises.

quiz.q: Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}\frac{x^2-16}{x-4} \). Direct substitution gives the indeterminate form 0/0.
quiz.solution: A 0/0 form signals a common factor, not a missing limit: \( x^2-16 = (x-4)(x+4) \), so the ratio equals \( x+4 \) everywhere except at \( x=4 \), and the limit is 8. Choosing "does not exist" is the classic division-by-zero reflex — the function value is undefined at 4, but a limit describes approach, not arrival, and it can exist exactly where the value does not. The answer 0 comes from reading a 0/0 form as literally zero; 16 comes from evaluating only the squared term after an illegal cancellation.

quiz.q: With \( \theta \) measured in radians, evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{\theta\to 0}\frac{\sin 3\theta}{\theta} \).
quiz.solution: Rescale so the sine's argument and the denominator vanish as the same quantity: \( \frac{\sin 3\theta}{\theta} = 3\cdot\frac{\sin 3\theta}{3\theta} \to 3\cdot 1 = 3 \). Answering 1 is the classic pattern-match error: the standard limit \( \sin u/u \to 1 \) applies only when numerator argument and denominator are identical, which they are not here. The answer 0 comes from substituting \( \theta = 0 \) into the numerator alone, and 1/3 from performing the rescaling upside-down; note the whole computation also presumes radians, since the standard limit is false in degrees.

quiz.q: Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\frac{|x|}{x} \).
quiz.solution: From the right, \( |x|/x = 1 \); from the left, \( |x|/x = -1 \). The one-sided limits disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist — the graph is a genuine step. This is the archetype of threshold behavior: whenever an expression contains an absolute value or a piecewise switch, check the sides separately before declaring a limit. Answering 1 or −1 reports only one approach direction; answering 0 averages two worlds the function never occupies.

quiz.q: A compressor trips and the check valve on its discharge line slams shut. Four plant variables are logged at a 1 s scan. Which one can genuinely change as a step at the trip instant — a true physical discontinuity rather than an instrument artifact?
choices: Casing metal temperature of the compressor
Line pressure downstream of the slamming check valve
Liquid level in the suction knockout drum
Pipe-wall temperature of the discharge line
quiz.solution: A slamming valve imposes a pressure step that is genuine at any practical scan rate — pressure is the lesson's example of a physical quantity that can truly jump. Metal temperatures and liquid level are accumulations of finite fluxes (heat through thermal mass, flow into a vessel), so their true trajectories are continuous, with time constants far longer than one scan. Selecting a temperature channel is the classic error of mistaking a logged jump for a process event: a step in a thermally massive variable indicates an instrument artifact, exactly as in the pasteurizer RTD case.

quiz.q: The function \( g(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1} \) is undefined at \( x = 1 \). What kind of discontinuity lives there?
choices: Removable — the limit exists (it is 2) but the definition misses the point
A jump — the one-sided limits disagree
None — the function is continuous at 1
quiz.solution: Factoring gives \( g(x) = x + 1 \) for \( x \ne 1 \), so both one-sided limits equal 2: the graph is a straight line with a single hole at \( (1, 2) \) — a removable discontinuity. It is not a jump (the sides agree) and not a blow-up (nothing grows without bound); calling it continuous confuses the limit existing with the value existing, which is exactly the distinction this lesson draws.

3.lecture:
§1 · From average rate to instantaneous rate

Between Monday and Friday a bearing housing warmed from 62 °C to 74 °C: average rate 3 °C/day. But right now, this hour, how fast is it heating? Squeeze the interval and the average rate converges — Lesson 2's limit — to the instantaneous rate:

\[ \frac{dT}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{T(t+\Delta t)-T(t)}{\Delta t} \tag{1} \]

That is the derivative: the local slope of the trend, the speed of change at an instant. Units divide: °C per hour, m³ per second, mm/s of vibration per week. A derivative always has units, and always tells a maintenance story.

§2 · The derivative as the trend's confession

Condition monitoring is derivative-reading as a profession. A flat vibration trend with \( dV/dt \approx 0 \) buys you time; the same amplitude with a steep positive slope buys you a work order. Two machines at identical 4 mm/s are in different worlds if one arrived there over three years and the other over three weeks. Value tells you where it is; the derivative tells you where it's going.

§3 · Computing from a model

Given a fitted trend \( T(t) = 40 + 0.8t + 0.05t^2 \) (°C, hours), the power rule (proved next lesson) gives \( T'(t) = 0.8 + 0.1t \): the heating accelerates, because the rate itself grows. Set \( T'(t) \) against an alarm rate and you get a time-to-act, not just a number: solve \( 0.8+0.1t = 2.5 \) → \( t = 17\ \mathrm{h} \). The derivative converts a curve into a deadline.

§4 · Computing derivatives from data — the working method

Real trends arrive as samples, not formulas, so the working derivative is a finite difference. From the two latest points you can only form the forward-looking secant \( (f_{now} - f_{prev})/\Delta t \); mid-record, prefer the central difference \( (f_{i+1} - f_{i-1})/(2\Delta t) \), which cancels the first layer of error and tracks the true tangent far better on smooth data. Three disciplines make the estimates trustworthy. State the units — a slope is always something per something, and writing mm/s per week forces the physical meaning into view. Respect noise: differentiation amplifies it, so difference over intervals long enough for the real change to dominate the scatter; a derivative computed from two noisy readings an hour apart is an opinion, not a measurement. Distinguish model from data: when a curve has been fitted, differentiate the model symbolically and use the data-side difference as its check — when the two disagree badly, the fit, the data, or the machine has changed, and every one of those is worth knowing.

Then read the result the way §2 taught: the value says where the machine is, the derivative says where it is going, and the difference between those two sentences is the difference between recording a number and making a decision.

Closing. The derivative is a limit of average rates, it carries units, and on the plant floor it is the difference between watching a number and reading its intent.
3.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Limits (Lesson 2) — the derivative is literally a limit; no limit, no slope.
  • Slope of a line — rise over run; the derivative generalizes it to curves.
  • Function notation (Lesson 1) — \( T'(t) \) is itself a new function of time.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Derivative\( \tfrac{dT}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\tfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta t} \)The instantaneous rate; the slope of the trend at one instant, always with units.
Secant (average) rate\( \Delta T/\Delta t \)What two spot readings give; the derivative's finite approximation.
Forward difference\( (f_{i+1} - f_i)/h \)Rate from the latest two samples — the only choice at the record's live edge.
Central difference\( (f_{i+1} - f_{i-1})/(2h) \)The better mid-record estimate on smooth data; first-order errors cancel.
Rate-to-deadlinesolve \( f'(t) = \text{limit} \)Turning an alarm rate into a time-to-act.
Differentiabilityno corners, no jumpsKnowing where a slope exists at all; trend corners mark regime changes.
3.kuwait: Vignette — two identical numbers, one work order. You arrive for the monthly route at a cable plant's drawing line and both gearboxes read 3.9 mm/s. The history tells them apart: unit A has sat between 3.7 and 4.0 for two years (\( dV/dt \approx 0 \)); unit B was at 2.1 six weeks ago — a slope of ~0.3 mm/s per week and climbing. Same value, different derivative, different decision: A stays on route, B gets an inspection window before the next production campaign. When you defend that call in the morning meeting, the argument is equation (1), spoken in plain language: it isn't where the number is, it's how fast it's moving.

quiz.q:
Time-to-alarm from a fitted trend

Problem. A gearbox housing temperature trend fits \( T(t) = 40 + 0.8t + 0.05t^2 \) (°C, \(t\) in hours since the shift began). Site practice flags any heating rate above 2.5 °C/h. Find: the rate at \( t=10\ \mathrm{h} \) and the time the rate limit is reached.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Differentiate: \( T'(t) = 0.8 + 0.1t \). (2) At \( t=10 \): \( T'(10) = 0.8 + 1.0 = 1.8\ \mathrm{°C/h} \) — under the limit. (3) Set \( 0.8 + 0.1t = 2.5 \Rightarrow t = 17\ \mathrm{h} \).

Answer: 1.8 °C/h now; the 2.5 °C/h threshold arrives at \( t = 17\ \mathrm{h} \). Sanity check: the rate grows 0.1 °C/h every hour; from 1.8 at \(t=10\), seven more hours adds 0.7 → 2.5. Consistent.

quiz.q:
Filling rate from level

Problem. A vertical cylindrical tank, diameter 3.0 m, shows level rising at 0.40 m per minute. Find: the inflow in m³/h.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( V = A h \) with \( A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (3.0)^2/4 = 7.07\ \mathrm{m^2} \). (2) Chain of rates: \( \dot V = A \, \dot h = 7.07 \times 0.40 = 2.83\ \mathrm{m^3/min} \). (3) Convert: \( 2.83 \times 60 = 170\ \mathrm{m^3/h} \).

Answer: ≈170 m³/h. Sanity check: the tank gains 24 m³ every 10 minutes — about 3.4 m of level per hour, which a 3 m-diameter receiving tank plausibly does during transfer. Units chain cleanly: m² × m/min → m³/min.

quiz.q:
A derivative from historian samples

Problem. A vibration trend is sampled weekly: \( V = 2.0,\ 2.3,\ 2.9 \) mm/s at \( t = 0, 1, 2 \) weeks. Find: the rate of change at \( t = 1 \) week by forward difference and by central difference, with units, and state which estimate to trust on a smooth trend.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Forward difference: \( (2.9 - 2.3)/1 = 0.60 \) mm/s per week. (2) Central difference: \( (2.9 - 2.0)/2 = 0.45 \) mm/s per week. (3) On smooth data the central difference is the better tangent estimate — its first-order errors cancel; the forward difference looks half a step into the future and overstates a rising, accelerating trend.

Answer: forward 0.60, central 0.45 mm/s per week; prefer the central estimate mid-record. Sanity check: the weekly increments are 0.3 then 0.6 — the instantaneous rate at \( t=1 \) should sit between them, and 0.45 does.

quiz.q: A machine's vibration trend has been fitted as \( V(t) = 1.2 + 0.03t^2 \) (mm/s, with \( t \) in weeks since installation). What is the instantaneous rate of change of vibration at \( t = 5 \) weeks?
quiz.solution: Differentiating gives \( V'(t) = 0.06t \), so \( V'(5) = 0.30 \) mm/s per week. The distractor 0.15 comes from dropping the factor of 2 when differentiating \( t^2 \), and 1.95 is the value \( V(5) \) itself — confusing where the trend is with how fast it is moving. Answering 0.06 means the derivative was formed correctly but never evaluated at \( t = 5 \).

quiz.q: A pump bearing's temperature follows the fitted trend \( T(t) = 35 + 1.2t + 0.04t^2 \) (°C, \( t \) in hours since start-up). Site practice requires intervention once the heating rate reaches 3.0 °C/h. At what time does the rate reach that limit?
quiz.solution: The rate is \( T'(t) = 1.2 + 0.08t \); setting \( 1.2 + 0.08t = 3.0 \) gives \( t = 1.8/0.08 = 22.5 \) h — the derivative turns the curve into a deadline. The distractor 45 h comes from differentiating \( 0.04t^2 \) as \( 0.04t \) (dropping the factor of 2), and 37.5 h comes from dividing 3.0 by 0.08 without first subtracting the constant rate term 1.2 °C/h.

quiz.q: A bearing temperature \( T(t) \) is logged with \( T \) in °C and \( t \) in hours. What are the units of \( T'(t) \)?
quiz.solution: The derivative is a limit of \( \Delta T/\Delta t \), so its units are the ratio of the units: °C per hour. A derivative always carries units, and stating them is not pedantry — it is what turns "the trend is 1.8" into the actionable "the housing is heating at 1.8 °C every hour." Answering °C confuses the rate with the reading; dimensionless confuses mathematics with the pure-number world it models.

quiz.q: A recorded trend is continuous at \( t = t_0 \) but shows a sharp corner there, like \( |t - t_0| \)-shaped behaviour after a sudden load change. Which statement is correct?
choices: The function is continuous at \( t_0 \) but not differentiable there: the one-sided secant slopes converge to different values, so no single tangent slope exists.
Continuity at \( t_0 \) guarantees differentiability at \( t_0 \), so the derivative must exist.
The function is differentiable at \( t_0 \) but cannot be continuous there.
The derivative at \( t_0 \) exists and equals the average of the left-hand and right-hand slopes.
quiz.solution: At a corner the limit defining the derivative fails: the left and right difference quotients approach different values, yet the function value has no jump — continuous but not differentiable. The classic error is reversing the implication: differentiability implies continuity, never the converse. Averaging the two one-sided slopes is a tempting repair, but the defining limit simply does not exist, so no derivative can be assigned.

quiz.q: Between \( t = 2 \) h and \( t = 6 \) h a trend rises from 10 to 22 units — an average rate of 3 units/h. What can be said about the instantaneous rate at \( t = 4 \) h?
choices: It cannot be determined from the average alone
It is zero, since 4 h is the midpoint
quiz.solution: An average over an interval constrains nothing at any particular interior instant: the trend could be rising at 6 units/h at \( t=4 \) and crawling elsewhere, or vice versa. (The mean value theorem promises the instantaneous rate equals 3 somewhere in the interval — but not at any point you get to choose.) Reading an interval average as a point value is the classic trend-review error this lesson exists to retire.

4.lecture:
§1 · The rules — machinery for slopes

Yesterday's limit definition is correct and slow. Today we industrialize it. For constants \(c\), functions \(u, v\):

\[ (cu)' = cu', \qquad (u+v)' = u' + v', \qquad (x^n)' = nx^{n-1} \tag{1} \] \[ (uv)' = u'v + uv', \qquad \left(\frac{u}{v}\right)' = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2} \tag{2} \]

Each is proved once from the limit and used forever. The power rule follows from expanding \( (x+\Delta x)^n \) and watching every term above first order vanish with \( \Delta x \) — the same "higher-order terms die" argument that engineering approximation lives on.

§2 · The chain rule — rates through a chain of causes

Plant variables cause each other in chains: valve position sets flow, flow sets level, level sets pressure. If \( y = f(u) \) and \( u = g(x) \):

\[ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du}\cdot\frac{du}{dx} \tag{3} \]

Rates multiply along the chain. This single rule powers every sensitivity argument you will make: how much does discharge pressure move per percent of speed? Differentiate through the chain of dependencies and multiply the local sensitivities.

§3 · Relative rates — the engineer's favorite

Divide a derivative by its function and you get relative sensitivity. For a fan law \( P = kN^3 \), logarithmic differentiation gives \( \frac{dP}{P} = 3\frac{dN}{N} \): a 2% speed increase costs ≈6% more power ((1.02)³ = 1.061 exactly). This is why slowing a fan or pump slightly is the cheapest energy project in any plant — the cube works for you on the way down.

§4 · Choosing the rule — structure before symbols

Differentiation errors rarely come from forgetting a rule; they come from misreading the structure of the expression. So classify before you differentiate. Is the expression a sum of powers? The power rule handles each term independently — after rewriting roots and reciprocals as powers, \( \sqrt{x} = x^{1/2} \), \( 1/x = x^{-1} \). Is it two varying quantities multiplied — force times velocity, pressure times flow? That is a genuine product: product rule. A ratio with the variable in the denominator? Quotient rule, with the subtraction order fixed. A function of a function — something raised to a power, an exponential of an expression? Chain rule, differentiating outer times inner, working from the outside in.

For the power laws that fill engineering — \( P = kN^3 \), \( \Delta P = kQ^2 \) — logarithmic differentiation earns its keep: take logs, differentiate, and relative rates appear directly, \( dP/P = n\,dN/N \). That one line is the professional's sensitivity language: percent out per percent in. Keep the honest distinction from §3 visible — the relative-rate figure is a first-order estimate, exact only in the limit; for large changes, compute the ratio exactly and let the linear estimate serve as its sanity check.

Finally, make numerical verification a reflex. Any symbolic derivative can be audited in ten seconds: evaluate the function at \( x \) and at \( x + 0.001 \), divide the difference by 0.001, and compare with the formula's prediction. Agreement to three figures means the algebra survived; disagreement means a dropped inner derivative or a sign error is hiding in the work. Professionals check their derivatives the way welders check their fit-up — before the error is buried under everything built on top of it.

Closing. Rules (1)–(2) mechanize differentiation; the chain rule (3) walks rates through cause-and-effect; relative rates turn calculus into percentage talk that managers act on.
4.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The derivative as limit (Lesson 3) — today's rules are shortcuts to that limit.
  • Exponent algebra — \( x^{-1} \), \( x^{1/2} \) so the power rule covers roots and reciprocals.
  • Composite functions — recognizing \( f(g(x)) \) before differentiating it.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Power rule\( (x^n)' = n\,x^{n-1} \)Sums of powers — after rewriting roots and reciprocals as powers.
Product rule\( (uv)' = u'v + uv' \)Two varying factors multiplied: force × velocity, pressure × flow.
Quotient rule\( (u/v)' = (u'v - uv')/v^2 \)Ratios with the variable below the line; the subtraction order is fixed.
Chain rule\( \tfrac{dy}{dx} = \tfrac{dy}{du}\cdot\tfrac{du}{dx} \)Nested dependence; rates multiply along the cause chain.
Relative sensitivity\( \tfrac{dP}{P} = n\,\tfrac{dN}{N} \) for \( P = kN^n \)Percent-per-percent talk; affinity-law estimates and energy arguments.
Exact vs first-order\( (1+r)^n \) vs \( 1+nr \)Deciding when the linear estimate is honest enough — and checking it when it isn't.
4.kuwait: Vignette — selling the VFD with one line of calculus. You arrive at an energy review where a utilities fan farm runs throttled year-round. Management wants payback numbers, not mathematics. You write one line on the whiteboard — power moves three percent for every percent of speed — and translate: 10% slower ≈ 25–30% less power, per fan, forever. The chain rule and the cube law did the argument; the spreadsheet that follows merely confirms what equation (3) already promised. The retrofit gets scoped that week.

quiz.q:
Chain rule, straight up

Problem. \( y = (3x^2+2)^4 \). Find: \( y'(1) \).
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Outer: \( \frac{d}{du}u^4 = 4u^3 \) with \( u = 3x^2+2 \). (2) Inner: \( u' = 6x \). (3) Chain: \( y' = 4(3x^2+2)^3 \cdot 6x = 24x(3x^2+2)^3 \). (4) At \( x=1 \): \( 24 \times 5^3 = 24 \times 125 = 3000 \).

Answer: 3000. Sanity check: numerically, \( y(1.001) - y(1) \approx 628.0\!-\!625 = 3.0 \) over \( \Delta x = 0.001 \) → slope ≈ 3000. Confirmed.

quiz.q:
The affinity-law energy argument

Problem. A 90 kW cooling fan runs at fixed speed with dampers throttling. A VFD retrofit would slow it 10%. Using the fan law \( P = kN^3 \), find the approximate and exact power saving.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Relative rates: \( dP/P = 3\,dN/N = 3 \times (-10\%) = -30\% \) (first-order estimate). (2) Exact: \( (0.9)^3 = 0.729 \) → 27.1% saving. (3) Power: \( 90 \times 0.271 = 24.4\ \mathrm{kW} \) saved continuously.

Answer: ≈24 kW (exact 27.1%, linear estimate 30%). Sanity check: the linear estimate overshoots because the cube curve bends — for a 10% change the two agree within a few points, exactly what a first-order method promises.

quiz.q:
Power through the product rule

Problem. During a controlled winch lower-away, cable tension falls as \( F(t) = 200 - 5t \) N while payout speed rises as \( v(t) = 2 + 0.3t \) m/s. Mechanical power is \( P = Fv \). Find: \( P'(10) \) and interpret its sign.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Product rule: \( P' = F'v + Fv' = (-5)(2 + 0.3t) + (200 - 5t)(0.3) \). (2) At \( t = 10 \): \( (-5)(5) + (150)(0.3) = -25 + 45 = +20 \) W/s. (3) Interpretation: tension is falling, but the speed growth outweighs it — power is still rising at 20 watts per second.

Answer: \( P'(10) = +20 \) W/s; power increasing. Sanity check: \( P(10) = 150 \times 5 = 750 \) W and \( P(11) = 145 \times 5.3 = 768.5 \) W — a rise of 18.5 W over the next second, consistent with a +20 W/s instantaneous rate that is itself easing off.

By the chain rule, the outer derivative is \( 5u^4 \) with \( u = 2x^3 + 1 \) and the inner derivative is \( 6x^2 \), so \( y' = 30x^2(2x^3+1)^4 \), which at \( x = 1 \) gives \( 30 \times 3^4 = 2430 \). The distractor 405 is the classic error of differentiating only the outer power and forgetting to multiply by the inner derivative \( 6x^2 \). Writing the outer derivative as \( 5u^5 \) instead of \( 5u^4 \) — failing to reduce the exponent — produces 7290.

quiz.q: For differentiable functions \( u(x) \) and \( v(x) \) with \( v(x) \neq 0 \), which expression equals \( \left( \dfrac{u}{v} \right)' \)?
quiz.solution: The quotient rule is \( (u/v)' = (u'v - uv')/v^2 \): the term with the differentiated numerator comes first, and the subtraction order is fixed. Reversing the numerator to \( uv' - u'v \) is the classic sign error and yields exactly the negative of the true derivative. The plus-sign version transplants the product-rule pattern where it does not apply, and \( u'/v' \) — differentiating top and bottom separately — already fails on a case as simple as \( x/x^2 \).

Rewrite and apply the power rule: \( f'(x) = 4 \cdot \tfrac{3}{2}x^{1/2} = 6\sqrt{x} \), so \( f'(9) = 6 \times 3 = 18 \). Choosing 12 drops the \( 3/2 \) exponent factor; 162 forgets to lower the power (using \( x^{3/2} \) instead of \( x^{1/2} \)); 108 is \( f(9) \) itself — the value-versus-slope confusion. The power rule covers roots the moment you write them as fractional powers.

quiz.q: A centrifugal pump absorbs shaft power according to the affinity law \( P = kN^3 \), where \( N \) is rotational speed. A variable-frequency drive slows the pump by 8%. What is the exact percentage reduction in absorbed power?
quiz.solution: The exact power ratio is \( (0.92)^3 = 0.7787 \), so the saving is \( 1 - 0.7787 = 22.1\% \). The relative-rate estimate \( dP/P = 3\,dN/N = 24\% \) is only a first-order linearization; quoting it as the exact value is the classic error behind that distractor, and it overshoots because the cubic curve bends. Using the square law (which governs head, \( \propto N^2 \)) instead of the cube law for power gives 15.4%, while assuming power simply proportional to speed gives 8%.

quiz.q: Tank level responds to volume as \( dL/dV = 0.14 \) m per m³, and volume grows at \( dV/dt = 2.5 \) m³/min. How fast is level rising?
quiz.solution: The chain rule multiplies rates along the dependency chain: \( dL/dt = (dL/dV)(dV/dt) = 0.14 \times 2.5 = 0.35 \) m/min. Dividing (17.9) or adding (2.64) the sensitivities are the classic chain-rule confusions; 0.056 inverts one factor. When causes chain — valve to flow, flow to volume, volume to level — their local sensitivities multiply, which is the entire content of equation (3).

5.lecture:
§1 · The function that is its own derivative

One function grows at a rate equal to its own size: \( \frac{d}{dt}e^{t} = e^{t} \). Scale time by a constant and \( \frac{d}{dt}e^{kt} = k e^{kt} \): growth or decay proportional to the current amount. That single property makes \( e^{kt} \) the native language of charging capacitors, cooling castings, decaying vibration transients, draining accumulators, and compounding costs.

§2 · Decay with an offset — the plant's favorite curve

A hot object doesn't decay to zero; it decays to ambient. The working form is

\[ T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)\,e^{-t/\tau} \tag{1} \]

where \( \tau \) is the time constant: after one \( \tau \), 63% of the gap to ambient is closed; after 3\( \tau \), 95%; after 5\( \tau \), it's over. Ask "how many time constants?" and any first-order transient becomes readable at a glance.

§3 · Logarithms — solving for time

The logarithm inverts the exponential; it is how you extract when:

\[ t = \tau \,\ln\!\frac{T_0 - T_\infty}{T(t) - T_\infty} \tag{2} \]

Logs also compress scale (decibels, pH, Richter) and straighten exponentials: plot \( \ln(T - T_\infty) \) against \( t \) and first-order data falls on a line whose slope is \( -1/\tau \). Fitting a straight line to a log plot is how you measure a time constant from plant data.

§4 · The transient protocol

Every first-order transient yields to the same four questions, asked in order. What is the destination? Identify \( T_\infty \) — ambient, setpoint, asymptote — because everything is measured as a gap from it. What is the gap? Compute the current excess over the destination; the transient's whole story is this gap shrinking. What is the tempo? Get \( \tau \) — from a datasheet, from a fit, or from data via the semilog method of §3: plot \( \ln(\text{gap}) \) against time, and the slope is \( -1/\tau \). When? Turn any target into time with \( t = \tau\,\ln(\text{gap}_0/\text{gap}_{target}) \), or count in the coarse currency — one \( \tau \) closes 63% of the gap, three close 95%, five end the story.

The same machinery runs upward for growth. Compounding at rate \( r \) per period doubles in \( \ln 2/\ln(1+r) \) periods — the honest version of the rule of 70 — and any quantity that grows in proportion to itself, from an escalating tariff to a fouling deposit, is the exponential wearing different clothes. When data arrives, straighten it first: if the semilog plot is not a line, the process is not first-order, and no amount of \( \tau \)-talk will make it one. The plot is the test; the constants come after.

Know the honest failure modes too. A curve on the semilog plot usually means two time constants stacked — a fast surface effect riding on a slow bulk one — and a flat early section means deadtime: transport delay before the response begins. Both are information, not nuisance. Report what the data shows, fit the region the model genuinely describes, and say so; a stated approximation is engineering, an unstated one is luck.

Closing. \( e^{kt} \) encodes self-proportional change; \( \tau \) makes transients countable; logarithms solve for time and straighten curves so data confesses its constants.
5.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Chain rule (Lesson 4) — \( \tfrac{d}{dt}e^{-t/\tau} = -\tfrac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau} \).
  • Log laws — \( \ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b \), \( \ln e^x = x \).
  • Limits (Lesson 2) — \( e^{-t/\tau}\to 0 \): the transient's destiny is ambient.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Exponential change\( \tfrac{d}{dt}e^{kt} = k\,e^{kt} \)Any quantity whose rate is proportional to its size — growth or decay.
First-order transient\( T = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)e^{-t/\tau} \)Cooling, charging, draining — decay to an offset destination.
Time constant\( \tau \): 63% per \( \tau \), 95% by \( 3\tau \)Reading any transient's tempo at a glance.
Solving for time\( t = \tau\ln\tfrac{T_0 - T_\infty}{T - T_\infty} \)When the question is when, not what.
Half-life\( t_{1/2} = \tau\ln 2 \approx 0.693\tau \)Halving language for decays; never equal to \( \tau \) itself.
Semilog straightening\( \ln(T - T_\infty) \) vs \( t \), slope \( -1/\tau \)Measuring \( \tau \) from data — and testing whether first-order applies at all.
Doubling time\( n = \ln 2/\ln(1+r) \)Compounding costs, loads, and deposits.
5.kuwait: Vignette — how long until we can touch it? You arrive at a melt-shop planning meeting: a repair crew needs billets on the cooling bed handled "as soon as possible." The shift engineer guesses; you fit instead. Two historian points — 620 °C at 30 min, 259 °C at 60 min — on a semilog plot of \( T-30 \) give a straight line whose slope yields \( \tau \approx 45 \) min, and equation (2) turns that into a committed answer: just under two hours to 100 °C. Scheduling gets a number with a derivation behind it instead of a shrug, and nobody gloves up against a 260-degree surface.

quiz.q:
Billet cooling window

Problem. A steel billet leaves a cooling bed model at \( T_0 = 900\ \mathrm{°C} \) into \( T_\infty = 30\ \mathrm{°C} \) surroundings with \( \tau = 45 \) min (representative first-order fit). Find: temperature after 60 min, and the time to reach 100 °C for safe handling.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( T(60) = 30 + 870\,e^{-60/45} = 30 + 870\,e^{-1.333} \). (2) \( e^{-1.333} = 0.264 \Rightarrow T(60) = 30 + 229 = 259\ \mathrm{°C} \). (3) For 100 °C, equation (2): \( t = 45 \ln\frac{870}{70} = 45 \ln 12.43 = 45 \times 2.52 = 113\ \mathrm{min} \).

Answer: ≈259 °C after an hour; ≈113 min (≈2.5\( \tau \)) to 100 °C. Sanity check: 100 °C sits 92% of the way to ambient, and 2.5 time constants closes ~92% of the gap. Internally consistent.

quiz.q:
Doubling time from a growth rate

Problem. Plant electricity tariff escalates 3% per year. Find: the doubling time of the bill at constant consumption.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Growth: \( C = C_0(1.03)^n \). Doubling when \( (1.03)^n = 2 \). (2) \( n = \ln 2 / \ln 1.03 = 0.6931 / 0.02956 = 23.4 \) years.

Answer: ≈23 years. Sanity check: the rule of 70 gives \( 70/3 \approx 23.3 \) — the shortcut and the logarithm agree.

quiz.q:
A fouling filter on the calendar

Problem. A filter's differential pressure grows 5% per week from a clean-condition \( \Delta P_0 = 0.30 \) bar. The changeout alarm is set at 0.60 bar. Find: how many weeks until the alarm, assuming the compounding trend holds.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Model: \( \Delta P = 0.30\,(1.05)^n \). Alarm when \( (1.05)^n = 2 \). (2) Take logarithms: \( n = \ln 2/\ln 1.05 = 0.6931/0.04879 = 14.2 \) weeks.

Answer: ≈14 weeks — a doubling time, read straight off the growth rate. Sanity check: the rule of 70 gives \( 70/5 = 14 \) — shortcut and logarithm agree, so the maintenance plan can commit to a changeout window around week 13 with margin.

quiz.q: A thermocouple with first-order time constant \( \tau = 8\ \mathrm{s} \) reads 20 °C when it is plunged into an oil bath held at 80 °C. What does it read 12 s later?
quiz.solution: The first-order response is \( T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)e^{-t/\tau} = 80 - 60\,e^{-1.5} \approx 66.6 \) °C. The 57.8 °C distractor applies the one-time-constant 63% rule to an interval of \( 1.5\tau \); that figure holds only at exactly one \( \tau \). 110 °C extrapolates the initial rate linearly, ignoring that the driving gap shrinks as the reading approaches the bath, and 33.4 °C swaps the roles of \( T_0 \) and \( T_\infty \).

quiz.q: A casting's cooling transient is logged and \( \ln(T - T_\infty) \) is plotted against time; the points fall on a straight line of slope \( -0.040\ \mathrm{min^{-1}} \). What is the half-life of the transient, i.e. the time for the remaining temperature gap to halve?
quiz.solution: The semilog slope equals \( -1/\tau \), so \( \tau = 1/0.040 = 25 \) min, and the half-life is \( t_{1/2} = \tau \ln 2 \approx 17.3 \) min. Choosing 25 min confuses the time constant (63% of the gap closed) with the half-life (50% closed). 36.1 min inverts the relation as \( \tau/\ln 2 \), and 12.5 min guesses \( \tau/2 \) in place of \( \tau \ln 2 \).

quiz.q: A first-order transient has run for exactly three time constants. Roughly what fraction of the initial gap to the destination remains?
quiz.solution: The remaining fraction decays as \( e^{-t/\tau} \); at \( t = 3\tau \) that is \( e^{-3} \approx 0.050 \) — about 5% left, 95% closed. Answering 63% or 37% swaps the closed and remaining fractions at one time constant into the wrong slot; 95% states what is done, not what remains. The 63/95/99 ladder at \( 1\tau/3\tau/5\tau \) is worth owning cold — it is the transient-reading currency of every discipline downstream.

quiz.q: You must confirm that a cooling trend is first-order and extract its time constant graphically. Which plot renders the data straight, and what does its slope encode?
choices: \( \ln T \) versus \( t \); the slope is \( -1/\tau \)
\( \ln(T - T_\infty) \) versus \( t \); the slope is \( -1/\tau \)
\( \ln(T - T_\infty) \) versus \( t \); the slope is \( -\tau \)
\( T \) versus \( \ln t \); the slope is \( -1/\tau \)
quiz.solution: Since \( T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty)e^{-t/\tau} \), taking logarithms gives \( \ln(T - T_\infty) = \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) - t/\tau \): a straight line in \( t \) with slope \( -1/\tau \). Plotting \( \ln T \) without subtracting ambient is the classic error — the offset \( T_\infty \) leaves the curve bent, because the pure exponential is the gap, not the temperature itself. A slope of \( -\tau \) inverts the fingerprint, and \( T \) versus \( \ln t \) straightens logarithmic trends, not exponential ones.

quiz.q: For a first-order transient, how long does closing 99% of the gap take, expressed in time constants?
quiz.solution: Requiring \( e^{-t/\tau} = 0.01 \) gives \( t = \tau\ln 100 \approx 4.6\,\tau \) — the origin of the field rule that five time constants end the story. \( \tau\ln 10 \) closes only 90%; \( \tau\ln 2 \) is the half-life; and \( 99\tau \) misreads the exponential as linear, the very misreading the time-constant idea exists to prevent.

6.lecture:
§1 · Where curves turn

A smooth function can only reverse direction where its derivative is zero. Those points — where \( f'(x) = 0 \) — are the critical points, and among them live the maxima and minima that engineering actually cares about: least material, least energy, most throughput. The second derivative classifies them: \( f'' > 0 \) cups upward (minimum), \( f'' < 0 \) caps downward (maximum).

§2 · The optimization recipe

Every optimization in this course follows one recipe:

\[ \text{model} \to \text{constraint} \to \text{single-variable objective} \to f'(x)=0 \to \text{check ends} \tag{1} \]

Write the quantity to optimize; use the constraint to eliminate variables; differentiate; solve; then — the step everyone forgets — check the boundary. In a plant, optima frequently live at the constraint (valve fully open, maximum allowed speed) rather than at an interior zero of the derivative. The mathematics is honest about this only if you ask.

§3 · A worked shape decision

Minimum-surface tanks, minimum-loss pipe runs, maximum-volume boxes from fixed sheet — these classic problems all reduce to recipe (1). In §Worked Examples we size an open-top square tank for 4 m³ with least plate area — the kind of trade a fabrication shop quotes weekly — and find the optimum has height equal to half the base side, a proportion worth memorizing.

§4 · From words to optimum — the translation discipline

The calculus in recipe (1) is the easy half; the profession lives in the translation that precedes it. Practice the sequence deliberately. Name the objective — the single quantity to minimize or maximize — and write its formula with every variable it depends on. Name the constraint — the fixed volume, the required duty, the forbidden region — as an equation, and use it to eliminate variables until the objective depends on one. Differentiate, set to zero, solve — then classify with the second derivative, and walk the boundary: evaluate the objective at every end of the allowed interval, because constrained optima are the plant's normal case, not its exception. Finally, sanity-check the geometry of the answer: optima usually land on proportions — height equals half the base, cost terms equal at the minimum — and a result that looks arbitrary is a result worth re-deriving.

Two honest cautions. A mathematical optimum is only as good as the model's costs: a plate that is cheapest by area may not be cheapest by welding length, and writing the model down makes that assumption visible and challengeable. And when the objective is nearly flat near its optimum — as it often is — precision fetishism wastes effort: report the flat region, not a false fourth digit.

Real decision variables are also rarely continuous. Batches come in integers, plate comes in stock sizes, speeds come in motor steps. The calculus still earns its keep: find the continuous optimum, then evaluate the objective at the feasible values on either side of it and take the better — the curve's flatness near the optimum usually makes the rounding cost negligible, and computing it proves that instead of assuming it.

Closing. Derivative zero finds candidates; the second derivative sorts them; the boundary check keeps you honest. Optimization is calculus with a purchase order attached.

  • Solving equations — critical points come from \( f'(x)=0 \), often polynomial.
